Claude Code Status Line Setup: Monitor Context Usage and Rate Limit in Real-Time
Claude Code Status Line setup: monitor context usage and rate limit in real-time

After using Claude Code for a while you've probably hit this — mid-conversation your Context gets compacted, or your Rate Limit silently gets used up, and you only find out when Claude Code tells you "you'll need to wait a bit".

Personally I used to run into this all the time. Context would suddenly get compacted, Claude would forget what we'd just discussed, and I'd have to re-explain everything. Pretty wasteful. The root cause is that Claude Code doesn't show this info on screen by default — you have no idea how much Context is left or how much of your Rate Limit you've burned through.

Good news though: Claude Code has a built-in feature called Status Line that puts this info at the bottom of your terminal in real time — current model, Context usage percentage, 5-hour and 7-day Rate Limit usage, and so on. Once it's set up, no more guessing. Let's walk through how to configure it.
In short, Status Line is a status bar at the bottom of your terminal that shows live info about your current Claude Code session.

The mechanism is straightforward: Claude Code passes the session's data as JSON via stdin to a script you specify, your script parses it and prints one line of text, and Claude Code displays that line at the bottom of the terminal.
So Claude Code provides the data, and you decide what to display by writing the script.
The JSON Claude Code passes to the script includes the following fields:
The Rate Limit fields only carry data for Claude.ai Pro/Max subscribers. If you log in with an API key, those fields will be empty.
The fastest way to set it up
If you don't want to write the script yourself, Claude Code provides a handy command. Just type this in the Claude Code input box:
language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
/statusline show model name, context remaining percentage, and 5h and 7d rate limit usage
Claude Code will generate the script and update the config for you — no manual work needed.
After running it, Claude Code generates a default script in p10k-ish style that looks roughly like this:

#!/bin/sh
# Claude Code status line script
# Mirrors the p10k prompt style: dir | git | model | context

input=$(cat)

# Directory: shorten home to ~
cwd=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.workspace.current_dir // .cwd // ""')
short_dir=$(echo "$cwd" | sed "s|^$HOME|~|")

# Git branch (skip optional locks)
git_branch=""
if [ -d "$cwd/.git" ] || git -C "$cwd" rev-parse --git-dir > /dev/null 2>&1; then
  git_branch=$(git -C "$cwd" -c core.useBuiltinFSMonitor=false symbolic-ref --short HEAD 2>/dev/null)
fi

# Model short name
model=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.model.display_name // ""')

# Context remaining
remaining=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.context_window.remaining_percentage // empty')

# Build output
parts=""

# Directory segment (cyan)
parts=$(printf '\033[36m%s\033[0m' "$short_dir")

# Git branch segment (green)
if [ -n "$git_branch" ]; then
  parts="$parts $(printf '\033[32m\033[0m \033[32m%s\033[0m' "$git_branch")"
fi

# Model segment (yellow)
if [ -n "$model" ]; then
  parts="$parts $(printf '\033[33m%s\033[0m' "$model")"
fi

# Context remaining (dimmed, only when available)
if [ -n "$remaining" ]; then
  remaining_int=$(printf '%.0f' "$remaining")
  if [ "$remaining_int" -le 20 ]; then
    # Warning: red when low
    parts="$parts $(printf '\033[31mctx:%s%%\033[0m' "$remaining_int")"
  else
    parts="$parts $(printf '\033[2mctx:%s%%\033[0m' "$remaining_int")"
  fi
fi

printf '%s' "$parts"
# Mirrors the p10k prompt style: dir | git | model | context
You can see the default script already handles a few things:
: dim grey by default, automatically turns red as a warning when below 20%
The overall style mirrors the Powerlevel10k (p10k) terminal theme — if you're already on p10k, it'll feel consistent.
If you don't know what Powerlevel10k is, check out my earlier post
Mac OS 終端機美化看這一篇就夠了！
(article in Mandarin) for a full install and setup guide.
The default script is fine for most people, but if — like me — you're using Powerlevel10k (p10k), you'll spot a problem:
the working directory and Git branch are already in the p10k prompt
. Showing them again in Status Line is just wasted space.
And more importantly, the default script is missing a few things I think are the most useful:
: This is where you actually trip — by the time you find out the 5-hour bucket is empty, it's too late
: The default only has dim and red, no yellow middle zone. By the time it goes red you're often already past the point of doing something about it
: For developers using API keys, knowing how much the current session has cost is genuinely useful in real time
So next I'll share the customised version I use. Take it as-is or tweak it for your own needs.
Before writing the script I thought about which info is "only Status Line can show me":
So my version focuses on these. I'll also show how to add the directory and Git branch back in later.
For colours, I went with three stages:
(21% - 49%): Heads up, start wrapping up or prepping a fresh session
(<= 20%): Danger, finish what you're doing fast
The same logic applies to both Context and Rate Limit, so a glance tells you exactly where you stand.

#!/bin/sh
input=$(cat)

# ── Nerd Fonts icons ──
# If your terminal doesn't have Nerd Fonts installed, swap these for plain text
# e.g. ICON_CTX="ctx", ICON_CLOCK="⏰", ICON_COST="$"
ICON_CTX=$(printf '\uf2db')     # chip icon
ICON_CLOCK=$(printf '\uf017')   # clock icon
ICON_COST=$(printf '\uf155')    # dollar icon

# ── Read input ──
model=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.model.display_name // ""')
ctx_remain=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.context_window.remaining_percentage // empty')
five_h_used=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.rate_limits.five_hour.used_percentage // empty')
seven_d_used=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.rate_limits.seven_day.used_percentage // empty')
cost=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.cost.total_cost_usd // empty')

# ── Colour by remaining percentage ──
# Green >=50% │ Yellow 21-49% │ Red <=20%
color_by_remain() {
  local val=$1
  if [ "$val" -le 20 ]; then
    printf '\033[31m'   # red
  elif [ "$val" -le 49 ]; then
    printf '\033[33m'   # yellow
  else
    printf '\033[32m'   # green
  fi
}

# ── Mini progress bar: 10 cells wide ──
mini_bar() {
  local percent=$1
  local width=10
  local filled=$((percent * width / 100))
  local empty=$((width - filled))
  local i=0
  while [ $i -lt $filled ]; do printf '━'; i=$((i + 1)); done
  while [ $i -lt $width ]; do printf '┄'; i=$((i + 1)); done
}

# ── Separator ──
SEP=$(printf '\033[2m │ \033[0m')

# ── Build output ──
parts=""

# Model (purple bold, set apart from the rest)
if [ -n "$model" ]; then
  parts=$(printf '\033[1;35m%s\033[0m' "$model")
fi

# Context remaining (progress bar + percentage)
if [ -n "$ctx_remain" ]; then
  val=$(printf '%.0f' "$ctx_remain")
  color=$(color_by_remain "$val")
  bar=$(mini_bar "$val")
  parts="$parts$SEP$(printf '%s%s %s %s%%\033[0m' "$color" "$ICON_CTX" "$bar" "$val")"
fi

# 5-hour Rate Limit
if [ -n "$five_h_used" ]; then
  val=$((100 - $(printf '%.0f' "$five_h_used")))
  color=$(color_by_remain "$val")
  parts="$parts$SEP$(printf '%s%s 5h:%s%%\033[0m' "$color" "$ICON_CLOCK" "$val")"
fi

# 7-day Rate Limit
if [ -n "$seven_d_used" ]; then
  val=$((100 - $(printf '%.0f' "$seven_d_used")))
  color=$(color_by_remain "$val")
  parts="$parts$SEP$(printf '%s%s 7d:%s%%\033[0m' "$color" "$ICON_CLOCK" "$val")"
fi

# Session cost (dim — visible when needed, not loud)
if [ -n "$cost" ] && [ "$cost" != "0" ] && [ "$cost" != "null" ]; then
  parts="$parts$SEP$(printf '\033[2m%s $%s\033[0m' "$ICON_COST" "$cost")"
fi

printf '%s' "$parts"
# If your terminal doesn't have Nerd Fonts installed, swap these for plain text
# Model (purple bold, set apart from the rest)
# Session cost (dim — visible when needed, not loud)

Restart Claude Code and you'll see the live status info at the bottom of the terminal.
Colours change based on remaining percentage — green for safe, yellow when you should pay attention, red for almost out. Once it goes red, run
to compress Context, or just open a fresh session.
A quick summary of each segment and its colour logic:
Always shown, visually distinct from the rest
10-cell progress bar + percentage, the most intuitive view
Remaining quota in the rolling 5-hour window
Remaining quota in the rolling 7-day window
Rate Limit and Session cost fields will have different data depending on how you log in. Pro/Max subscribers get Rate Limit data, API key users get Session cost data. The script uses
to check, so empty fields just don't render — no blank segments to worry about.

# Working directory (cyan with folder icon)

fi
if [ -n "$git_branch" ]; then

Two things to watch for here:
: The directory and Git branch sections already populated
. If you reset it here, everything before gets wiped out
: The original was a plain assignment
, which would also overwrite. Switch to append so it joins on to what's already there
Full version (with directory and Git branch)
If you'd rather not piece it together yourself, here's the finished version — copy, paste, done:

#!/bin/sh
input=$(cat)

# ── Nerd Fonts icons ──
# Without Nerd Fonts, replace icons with plain text or emoji
# e.g. ICON_DIR="📁", ICON_GIT="🔀", ICON_CTX="ctx", ICON_CLOCK="⏰", ICON_COST="$"
ICON_DIR=$(printf '\uf07b')     # folder icon
ICON_GIT=$(printf '\ue725')     # Git branch icon
ICON_CTX=$(printf '\uf2db')     # chip icon (Context)
ICON_CLOCK=$(printf '\uf017')   # clock icon (Rate Limit)
ICON_COST=$(printf '\uf155')    # dollar icon (cost)

# ── Read input ──
model=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.model.display_name // ""')
ctx_remain=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.context_window.remaining_percentage // empty')
five_h_used=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.rate_limits.five_hour.used_percentage // empty')
seven_d_used=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.rate_limits.seven_day.used_percentage // empty')
cost=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.cost.total_cost_usd // empty')

# ── Colour by remaining percentage ──
# Green >=50% │ Yellow 21-49% │ Red <=20%
color_by_remain() {
  local val=$1
  if [ "$val" -le 20 ]; then
    printf '\033[31m'   # red
  elif [ "$val" -le 49 ]; then
    printf '\033[33m'   # yellow
  else
    printf '\033[32m'   # green
  fi
}

# ── Mini progress bar: 10 cells wide ──
mini_bar() {
  local percent=$1
  local width=10
  local filled=$((percent * width / 100))
  local empty=$((width - filled))
  local i=0
  while [ $i -lt $filled ]; do printf '━'; i=$((i + 1)); done
  while [ $i -lt $width ]; do printf '┄'; i=$((i + 1)); done
}

# ── Separator ──
SEP=$(printf '\033[2m │ \033[0m')

# ── Working directory (cyan) ──
cwd=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.cwd // ""')
short_dir=$(echo "$cwd" | sed "s|^$HOME|~|")
parts=$(printf '\033[36m%s %s\033[0m' "$ICON_DIR" "$short_dir")

# ── Git branch (green) ──
git_branch=""
if [ -d "$cwd/.git" ] || git -C "$cwd" rev-parse --git-dir > /dev/null 2>&1; then
  git_branch=$(git -C "$cwd" -c core.useBuiltinFSMonitor=false symbolic-ref --short HEAD 2>/dev/null)
fi
if [ -n "$git_branch" ]; then
  parts="$parts$SEP$(printf '\033[32m%s %s\033[0m' "$ICON_GIT" "$git_branch")"
fi

# ── Model (purple bold) ──
if [ -n "$model" ]; then
  parts="$parts$SEP$(printf '\033[1;35m%s\033[0m' "$model")"
fi

# ── Context remaining (progress bar + percentage) ──
if [ -n "$ctx_remain" ]; then
  val=$(printf '%.0f' "$ctx_remain")
  color=$(color_by_remain "$val")
  bar=$(mini_bar "$val")
  parts="$parts$SEP$(printf '%s%s %s %s%%\033[0m' "$color" "$ICON_CTX" "$bar" "$val")"
fi

# ── 5-hour Rate Limit ──
if [ -n "$five_h_used" ]; then
  val=$((100 - $(printf '%.0f' "$five_h_used")))
  color=$(color_by_remain "$val")
  parts="$parts$SEP$(printf '%s%s 5h:%s%%\033[0m' "$color" "$ICON_CLOCK" "$val")"
fi

# ── 7-day Rate Limit ──
if [ -n "$seven_d_used" ]; then
  val=$((100 - $(printf '%.0f' "$seven_d_used")))
  color=$(color_by_remain "$val")
  parts="$parts$SEP$(printf '%s%s 7d:%s%%\033[0m' "$color" "$ICON_CLOCK" "$val")"
fi

# ── Session cost (dim grey) ──
if [ -n "$cost" ] && [ "$cost" != "0" ] && [ "$cost" != "null" ]; then
  parts="$parts$SEP$(printf '\033[2m%s $%s\033[0m' "$ICON_COST" "$cost")"
fi

printf '%s' "$parts"
# Without Nerd Fonts, replace icons with plain text or emoji
# e.g. ICON_DIR="📁", ICON_GIT="🔀", ICON_CTX="ctx", ICON_CLOCK="⏰", ICON_COST="$"
If you'd rather not write a script at all, the community has a few ready-made options:
: Supports multiple themes and Powerline styles, highly customisable
: Comes with sane defaults, install and go
These are basically wrappers that generate the script for you, just without the writing step.
Why bother setting up Status Line?
You might be thinking: what's the point of knowing how much Context is left? Claude Code auto-compacts when it fills up anyway.

The thing is — compacting Context means Claude Code drops part of the previous conversation, and that can cause it to forget instructions or topics you discussed earlier. Then you re-explain, and the back-and-forth eats more time than the savings.
If you can see the remaining Context live, you can be proactive — run
when it's getting close to full, or open a fresh session, to avoid losing context.
Same goes for Rate Limit. If you know the 5-hour window is running out, you can prioritise the important task or pace differently, instead of getting blocked unexpectedly.
Status Line is a small but genuinely useful feature. Once it's set up you barely notice it's there, but when you need it, a glance tells you the current state — no more guessing.
If you're using Claude Code daily like I am, it's worth the few minutes to get this dialled in.

Tired of finding out your Context got compacted or your Rate Limit got eaten through only after the fact? This post walks through Claude Code's Status Line feature — showing model, remaining context, and rate limit usage live at the bottom of your terminal — plus a customised script with a progress bar and a three-stage colour warning. Copy, paste, done.
